Unveiling the spatial regulation of tumour progression in ovarian cancer

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

Sara Palomino-Echeverria1,2,3, Ada Junquera-Mencía1, Matías M. Falco1,4, Wenqing Chen1,4, Tatiana Belova3, Ceren Pajanoja5, Ping-an Hsieh3, Anna Vähärautio1,4, Marieke L. Kuijjer3,5,6*, Anniina Färkkilä1,5,7*

1: Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland: 2: Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM), Helsinki, Finland; 3: Computational Biology and Systems Medicine, Centre for Molecular Medicine Norway (NCMM), University of Oslo (UiO), Oslo, Norway; 4: Foundation for the Finnish Cancer Institute, Helsinki, Finland, 5: iCAN- Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland; 6: Department of Biochemistry and Developmental Biology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki; Finland, 7: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland ;*: These authors contributed equally to this work and share last authorship.

Multi-Omic Experimental Design and Spatial Profiling Pipeline for HGSC

Study Cohort and Multimodal Workflow. Fresh tumor tissue was collected from a cohort of 16 High-Grade Serous Carcinoma (HGSC) patients.

ign and Spatial Profiling Pipeline for HGSC

Study Cohort and Multimodal Workflow. Fresh tumor tissue was collected from a cohort of 16 High-Grade Serous Carcinoma (HGSC) patients. The cohort comprises patients undergoing both Primary (PDS, n=6) and Interval (IDS, n=9) Debulking Surgery, with a predominance of Homologous Recombination Proficient (HRP) tumors, and one deficient Platinum resistant. Samples were processed in parallel to enable comprehensive profiling: (1) Single-Cell Transcriptomics: Tissue dissociation followed by scRNA-seq. (2) Spatial Multi-Omics: Fixed FFPE blocks were analyzed using Xenium Immuno-Oncology (custom panel of 380+50 genes) to map gene expression in situ. The same tissue sections were subsequently subjected to low-plex t-CyCIF (16 protein markers) to spatially integrate proteomic with transcriptomic data. (3) Hierarchical integration of Xenium and t-CyCIF.

Single-Cell Resolution of Functional Tumor Heterogeneity and Spatial Organization in HGSC

Identification and Characterization of Distinct Tumor Cell States. A. Global Landscape and Tumor Subclustering. The UMAP (left) visualizes the major cell lineages across the HGSC cohort. Unsupervised clustering of the Tumor compartment (right) resolves six distinct functional phenotypes. B.

The UMAP (left) visualizes the major cell lineages across the HGSC cohort. Unsupervised clustering of the Tumor compartment (right) resolves six distinct functional phenotypes. B. Spatial-Molecular Correlation: The panel (left) correlates molecular identity with spatial distribution. The dot plot defines the gene signatures for each state at single cell level. Directly above, the corresponding Xenium spatial visualize the distinct tissue patterns and localization for each of these specific clusters. The right-hand panels validate these findings within the tissue context, showing the segmented tumor-stroma architecture (top) alongside the corresponding multiplexed fluorescence (bottom; DAPI, Membrane, RNA), confirming the physical arrangement of these cell states.

Visual correlation between RNA transcript density and corresponding protein expression levels. C. Spatial distribution of CCND1 transcripts (cyan dots) overlaid with anti-CCND1 protein immunofluorescence (magenta). D. MKI67 transcripts (orange dots) overlaid with anti-Ki-67 proliferation marker staining (red). The inset (bottom left) displays cell segmentation boundaries (white outlines), highlighting the correlation between intracellular transcript abundance and protein positivity within individual cells.

Cross-platform alignment and validation of spatial transcriptomics with immunofluorescence protein imaging

sara.palominoecheverria@helsinki.fi

Hsieh PH, Hsiao RX, Ferenc K, Mathelier A, Burkholz R, Chen CY, Sandve GK, Belova T, Kuijjer ML. (2024.) CAVACHON: a hierarchical variational autoencoder to integrate multi-modal single-cell data. ArXiv [cs.LG] arXiv:2405.18655

Hierarchical integration of spatial transcriptomics and proteomics could potentially enhance tumor cell resolution

Integrated transcriptomic–proteomic clustering using CAVACHON.

Hierarchical integration of spatial transcriptomics and proteomics could potentially enhance tumor cell resolution

Integrated transcriptomic–proteomic clustering using CAVACHON. By applying our hierarchical variational autoencoder (CAVACHON)1 to Xenium-derived gene expression and t-CycIF protein measurements, we aim to identify integrated clusters from a shared latent representation. By conditioning t-CycIF markers on Xenium genes, the model would potentially demonstrate robust performance even with reduced feature sets, highlighting its ability to capture subtle differences driven by post-transcriptional regulation.


1. Hsieh PH, Hsiao RX, Ferenc K, Mathelier A, Burkholz R, Chen CY, Sandve GK, Belova T, Kuijjer ML. (2024.) CAVACHON: a hierarchical variational autoencoder to integrate multi-modal single-cell data. ArXiv [cs.LG] arXiv:2405.18655
